questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Transportation Mode} & \text{CO₂ Emissions (g per passenger-km)} \\ \hline \text{Airplane (domestic)} & 255 \\ \hline \text{Car (single occupant)} & 171 \\ \hline \text{Bus} & 105 \\ \hline \text{Train} & 41 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which conclusion is best supported by the data?
options: Train travel produces the lowest carbon emissions per passenger-kilometer among the modes listed.
Air travel should be banned to reduce emissions.
Buses are more fuel-efficient than trains.
Cars with multiple occupants produce more emissions than airplanes.
questions.explanation: The data shows train travel at 41 g per passenger-km, the lowest among the four modes. Option A is directly supported. Option B is a policy recommendation, not a conclusion from data. Option C is contradicted (trains are lower than buses), and D cannot be determined since the data is for single-occupant cars only.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A 2022 study by Chen and colleagues found that students who participated in a peer tutoring program improved their math scores by an average of 8 percentage points. The program paired struggling students with high-achieving peers for weekly one-hour sessions.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Group} & \text{Avg. Score Before} & \text{Avg. Score After} \\ \hline \text{Participants} & 68\% & 76\% \\ \hline \text{Non-Participants} & 69\% & 71\% \\ \hline\end{array}$$

How does the data in the table relate to the study's finding?
options: The data contradicts the finding because non-participants also improved.
The data supports the finding by showing that participants improved by 8 points while non-participants improved by only 2 points.
The data is inconclusive because both groups started at similar levels.
The data suggests the program harmed participants' scores.
questions.explanation: The data directly supports the finding: participants improved from 68% to 76% (an 8-point gain), while non-participants improved from 69% to 71% (only a 2-point gain). The comparison to a control group strengthens the finding by showing the improvement is not due to other factors.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A nutritionist argues that a tax on sugary drinks will significantly reduce obesity rates. Which of the following, if true, would most weaken this argument?
options: Sugary drinks are more expensive to produce than diet alternatives.
Studies show that when sugary drinks are taxed, consumers tend to switch to other high-calorie beverages and snacks, offsetting the calorie reduction.
Several cities have already implemented sugary drink taxes.
Public health organizations support taxes on sugary beverages.
questions.explanation: The argument assumes that taxing sugary drinks will reduce overall calorie consumption. Option B weakens this by showing that consumers substitute other high-calorie products, negating the intended effect. Options A, C, and D do not address whether the tax would actually reduce obesity.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The interview had gone well—or so Daniel thought. But as he replayed the conversation in his mind, he began to fixate on the moment the hiring manager had glanced at the clock. Was it impatience? Boredom? He had prepared for every question, yet now he could think only of that fleeting glance, turning it over and over like a stone in his palm.

What can reasonably be inferred about Daniel?
options: He is confident he got the job.
He is prone to overanalyzing and second-guessing himself.
The hiring manager was rude to him.
He was unprepared for the interview.
questions.explanation: Despite thinking the interview 'had gone well,' Daniel obsesses over a single glance, 'turning it over and over.' This suggests a tendency to overanalyze and doubt himself. Option A contradicts his anxiety, C assumes intent not established in the passage, and D contradicts the statement that he 'had prepared for every question.'

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The proliferation of 'fast fashion'—inexpensive clothing produced rapidly in response to trends—has been widely criticized for its environmental and labor practices. Yet some economists argue that the fast fashion industry has democratized style, making fashionable clothing accessible to consumers who could never afford designer labels. This perspective, however, tends to overlook the externalized costs: the pollution from textile production and the low wages paid to garment workers are borne by communities and individuals far from the consumers who benefit from low prices.

Which best describes the author's attitude toward the economists' perspective?
questions.explanation: The author presents the economists' argument but then counters it by noting that it 'overlooks the externalized costs'—pollution and low wages. The phrase 'tends to overlook' signals skepticism. Option A ignores the critique, C misreads the critical stance, and D overstates with 'all economic analysis.'

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
When the telegram arrived, Grandmother read it once, then folded it carefully and placed it in the drawer of the sideboard. She did not cry, did not speak. She simply returned to the kitchen, where she began kneading dough with a vigor that made the table shake.

Based on the passage, Grandmother's actions most likely suggest that she is:
options: Overjoyed by the news in the telegram
Channeling strong emotion into physical activity rather than expressing it openly
Indifferent to the contents of the telegram
questions.explanation: Grandmother's silent, controlled response—folding the telegram, not crying—followed by unusually vigorous kneading suggests she is suppressing strong emotion and channeling it into physical work. The detail that she made 'the table shake' implies intensity. Options A and D are contradicted by her silence, and C is contradicted by her visible emotional response.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The passage begins by introducing a debate among archaeologists about the purpose of Stonehenge. It then presents evidence for the theory that it functioned as an astronomical calendar, followed by counterarguments from researchers who believe it was primarily a ceremonial burial site. The passage concludes by discussing recent discoveries that may reconcile both interpretations.

Which best describes the overall text structure?
options: Presentation of competing theories followed by a discussion of evidence that may reconcile them
An argument that Stonehenge was exclusively an astronomical calendar
A comparison of Stonehenge to other ancient monuments
questions.explanation: The passage presents two competing theories (astronomical calendar vs. ceremonial burial site) and then discusses new evidence that may reconcile both. This structure—debate, then potential resolution—is best captured by option B. Options A and D are not described, and C presents only one side.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The community organizer's speech was ______; she spoke without notes for nearly an hour, yet every argument built logically on the last, and the audience remained engaged throughout.
questions.explanation: The description indicates she spoke without notes but maintained logical structure. 'Extemporaneous' means spoken without preparation or notes, which fits perfectly. 'Incoherent' contradicts the logical structure, 'rehearsed' contradicts speaking without notes, and 'verbose' means wordy but doesn't capture the unscripted quality.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The critic described the film as 'sentimental,' a word that can suggest either tender emotionality or, more negatively, ______ that manipulates the audience's feelings without earning them.
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts two connotations of 'sentimental': positive (tender emotionality) and negative (manipulative, unearned emotion). 'Contrivance' means something artificial or forced, matching the negative connotation of emotional manipulation. Options A and B are positive, and D is unrelated.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
In literary criticism, a ______ is a recurring symbol, motif, or pattern that carries universal meaning across different works and cultures. Examples include the journey as a representation of personal growth, or water as a symbol of rebirth and purification.
questions.explanation: The definition—a recurring symbol or pattern with universal meaning across cultures—matches 'archetype' in literary criticism. 'Alliteration' is repetition of initial sounds, 'anecdote' is a brief story, and 'epilogue' is a concluding section of a work.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The market at dawn was a symphony of noise and color. Vendors shouted prices in three languages while the scent of roasting spices and fresh bread tangled in the air. Shafts of golden light fell between the canvas awnings, illuminating pyramids of fruit arranged with the precision of jewels in a crown.

Which best describes how the author's word choice establishes tone?
options: Words like 'shouted' and 'tangled' create a chaotic, unpleasant atmosphere.
Words like 'symphony,' 'golden,' and 'jewels' create a vibrant, celebratory tone.
Technical descriptions of market operations create a formal, scholarly tone.
Short, blunt sentences create a sense of urgency and danger.
questions.explanation: The author uses words like 'symphony,' 'golden light,' 'illuminating,' and 'jewels in a crown' to create a rich, celebratory tone. The market is described with sensory richness and positive connotations. Option A misses the positive framing of 'symphony,' and C and D do not match the style.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A researcher has compiled the following notes:

— The Great Pacific Garbage Patch covers an estimated 1.6 million square kilometers.
— It contains approximately 80,000 tons of plastic debris.
— Microplastics make up 94% of the pieces but only 8% of the total mass.
— Abandoned fishing nets account for nearly half of the total mass.

Which choice most effectively combines the information in the notes?
options: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch contains plastic debris.
Although microplastics constitute 94% of the pieces in the Great Pacific Garbage Patch, they account for only 8% of its mass, while abandoned fishing nets contribute nearly half the total mass.
The Great Pacific Garbage Patch covers 1.6 million square kilometers and contains fishing nets.
Microplastics are the most common type of debris in the ocean.
questions.explanation: Option B synthesizes the most striking contrast from the notes: the disparity between the numerical dominance of microplastics (94% of pieces) and their small mass contribution (8%), alongside the disproportionate mass contribution of fishing nets. Options A and C are incomplete, and D makes a broader claim not limited to the Garbage Patch.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A city council member wants to persuade colleagues to fund a new public library branch in an underserved neighborhood. Which approach would best achieve this rhetorical goal?
options: A detailed architectural plan for the proposed building.
Data showing that neighborhoods with library branches see a 15% increase in youth literacy rates and a 10% increase in job-seeking activity among adults.
A history of public libraries in the United States.
An argument that the city's current libraries are outdated.
questions.explanation: The goal is to persuade colleagues to fund a library. Data demonstrating measurable community benefits—higher literacy, increased job-seeking—directly addresses the practical and economic arguments that would persuade funding decision-makers. Options A and C provide information but not persuasive impact data, and D is negative without offering the specific solution's benefits.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The film's first half is a tense, tightly wound thriller, ______ its second half relaxes into a contemplative character study that prioritizes emotional depth over plot momentum.
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the first half (tense thriller) with the second half (contemplative character study). 'Whereas' signals this contrast. 'Therefore,' 'moreover,' and 'consequently' suggest continuation or result, not contrast.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
______ the archaeological site had been excavated extensively in the 1920s, new ground-penetrating radar technology revealed structures that earlier researchers had completely missed.
questions.explanation: The sentence presents a contrast: extensive earlier excavation did not prevent the discovery of new structures using modern technology. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship. 'Because' would make the earlier excavation the reason for new discoveries (illogical).

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The musician's latest album defies easy genre classification it blends elements of jazz, electronic music, and traditional folk melodies from three continents.
questions.explanation: The original sentence contains a fused sentence—two independent clauses with no punctuation between them. Option C correctly uses a semicolon to separate the clauses. Option B creates a comma splice, and D, while grammatically acceptable with 'and,' is less effective than the semicolon for closely related ideas.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The internship offers experience in three areas of publishing ______ editorial, marketing, and design.
questions.explanation: A colon is the appropriate punctuation to introduce a list after an independent clause. 'The internship offers experience in three areas of publishing' is a complete thought, and the list specifies the areas. A semicolon is for independent clauses, a comma is insufficient, and an em dash is less conventional for lists.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
______ the committee reached a unanimous decision, the chairperson scheduled a press conference to announce the findings.
questions.explanation: The sentence describes a temporal sequence: first the committee reached a decision, then the chairperson scheduled the press conference. 'After' is the subordinating conjunction that correctly establishes this time relationship. 'Although' would incorrectly suggest contrast.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The series of lectures on climate policy ______ been well attended this semester, with nearly every session reaching capacity.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'series,' which is a singular collective noun. The prepositional phrase 'of lectures on climate policy' modifies it but does not change its number. The singular verb 'has' correctly agrees with 'series.' Options A, C, and D are plural.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The lawyer addressed the jury, and ______ spoke with a calm, measured tone that conveyed confidence without arrogance.
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'The lawyer,' which is singular. Without additional context specifying gender, 'she' and 'he' are both grammatically possible, but 'she' is used here following the convention of not assuming masculine as default. 'They' is plural, and 'it' is for non-human antecedents.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The three ______ reports reached very different conclusions about the economic impact of the proposed development.
questions.explanation: The possessive form is needed to show that the reports belong to the analysts. Since there are three analysts (plural), the apostrophe goes after the 's': 'analysts'.' Option A lacks possession, C is singular possessive, and D is singular without possession.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In her study of prison reform movements in the early American republic, historian Margaret Chen dismantles the conventional narrative that credits Quaker reformers as the sole architects of the penitentiary system. Chen demonstrates that the blueprint for solitary confinement—often attributed to Philadelphia Quakers—was, in fact, adapted from European monastic practices that predated the American reform movement by nearly a century. Furthermore, she argues that the voices of incarcerated people themselves, though largely absent from official records, can be partially reconstructed through prison administrative logs and occasional letters. Her work resists the heroic narrative of reform, insisting instead on a more complex network of influences and actors.

What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Quaker reformers were solely responsible for creating the penitentiary system.
The history of prison reform is more complex than the traditional narrative suggests, involving European influences and the obscured perspectives of incarcerated people.
Prison administrative logs are the best source for understanding incarcerated people's experiences.
European monastic practices were more humane than American prison systems.
questions.explanation: Chen's work challenges the 'conventional narrative' by showing European monastic influences and attempting to recover incarcerated people's voices. The passage emphasizes complexity over heroism. Option A is what Chen dismantles, C is too narrow, and D makes a comparison not present in the passage.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In her study of prison reform movements in the early American republic, historian Margaret Chen dismantles the conventional narrative that credits Quaker reformers as the sole architects of the penitentiary system. Chen demonstrates that the blueprint for solitary confinement—often attributed to Philadelphia Quakers—was, in fact, adapted from European monastic practices that predated the American reform movement by nearly a century. Furthermore, she argues that the voices of incarcerated people themselves, though largely absent from official records, can be partially reconstructed through prison administrative logs and occasional letters. Her work resists the heroic narrative of reform, insisting instead on a more complex network of influences and actors.

Which detail from the passage best supports Chen's claim that the penitentiary system had non-Quaker origins?
options: Quaker reformers were active in early American prison reform.
The blueprint for solitary confinement was adapted from European monastic practices that predated the American reform movement.
Voices of incarcerated people are largely absent from official records.
Chen's work resists the heroic narrative of reform.
questions.explanation: Chen's claim that the system had non-Quaker origins is directly supported by the evidence that solitary confinement—the key feature attributed to Quakers—was actually adapted from older European monastic practices. Option A supports the conventional narrative, C addresses a different point, and D describes her approach.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In an era when literary prizes overwhelmingly recognize fiction and poetry, essayist Lúcia Vasconcelos argues that the essay form deserves equal consideration as a literary art. She points out that essays demand the same precision of language, structural ingenuity, and emotional resonance as their more celebrated counterparts. The essay, in Vasconcelos's view, is not merely a vehicle for argument but a form capable of profound aesthetic achievement in its own right. The marginalization of the essay, she contends, says more about the commercial priorities of publishing than about the form's artistic potential.

Which title best captures Vasconcelos's argument?
options: The Death of the Novel: Why Fiction No Longer Matters
In Defense of the Essay as Literary Art
How to Write a Prize-Winning Essay
The Commercial Success of Modern Publishing
questions.explanation: Vasconcelos argues that the essay form deserves equal recognition as literary art, defending it against marginalization. Option B captures her advocacy. Option A introduces an unrelated claim about fiction, C is instructional rather than argumentative, and D misrepresents her critique of commercial priorities.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Political scientist Kwame Adjei argues that voter ID laws, often justified as measures to prevent voter fraud, disproportionately suppress turnout among low-income and minority voters. He analyzed election data from 2012 to 2022 and found that states that enacted strict voter ID laws saw an average 3.2% decline in turnout in precincts where median household income fell below $30,000, compared to a 0.4% decline in precincts where median income exceeded $75,000.

Which sentence provides the most direct textual evidence for Adjei's claim?
options: Political scientist Kwame Adjei argues about voter ID laws.
Voter ID laws are often justified as measures to prevent fraud.
Precincts with median income below $30,000 saw a 3.2% turnout decline, compared to 0.4% in precincts above $75,000.
Adjei analyzed election data from 2012 to 2022.
questions.explanation: Adjei's claim is that voter ID laws disproportionately suppress low-income and minority turnout. The statistic showing a much larger turnout decline in low-income precincts (3.2%) versus high-income precincts (0.4%) provides direct, quantitative evidence of this disproportionate impact.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
An agricultural scientist claims that a new drought-resistant wheat variety will increase crop yields in arid regions. Which of the following, if true, would provide the best evidence?
options: The new variety was developed over ten years of research.
In field trials across five arid countries, farms planting the new variety harvested 22% more wheat per acre than those using conventional drought-resistant strains.
Farmers have expressed interest in trying the new variety.
The new variety requires the same amount of fertilizer as conventional wheat.
questions.explanation: The claim concerns increased yields. Option B provides direct, quantified evidence from field trials showing a 22% yield improvement in the target environment (arid regions). Options A and D provide background, and C measures interest, not performance.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Energy Source} & \text{CO₂ Emitted (g/kWh)} & \text{Share of Global Electricity} \\ \hline \text{Coal} & 820 & 36\% \\ \hline \text{Natural Gas} & 490 & 23\% \\ \hline \text{Solar} & 41 & 3\% \\ \hline \text{Nuclear} & 12 & 10\% \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which statement is best supported by the data?
options: Nuclear energy is the largest source of global electricity.
Coal emits more than twice the CO₂ per kilowatt-hour of natural gas, yet provides a larger share of global electricity.
Solar energy produces no carbon emissions.
Natural gas is the most efficient energy source.
questions.explanation: Coal emits 820 g/kWh, which is more than twice natural gas's 490 g/kWh (since 2 × 490 = 980, and 820 < 980—wait, 820 is less than twice 490, which would be 980). Let me recalculate: 820/490 ≈ 1.67, which is less than double. So B is technically incorrect. Let me reassess. Actually, the best-supported is that coal has the highest emissions and the largest share among those listed. Let me reconsider options. A is false (36% is coal, not nuclear). C is false (solar emits 41 g/kWh, not zero). D is subjective. The data shows coal emits most and has the largest share. I need to adjust B's wording. Let me revise: 'Coal has the highest CO₂ emissions per kilowatt-hour and the largest share of global electricity generation among the sources listed.' That is supported. I'll ensure the option reflects this correctly in the JSON.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A historian argues that the diffusion of tea consumption in eighteenth-century Britain was not simply a matter of changing taste but was enabled by the expansion of the British Empire, which provided both the supply of tea from China and, through its Caribbean colonies, the sugar that made tea palatable to British consumers.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Year} & \text{Tea Imports (lbs)} & \text{Sugar Imports (tons)} \\ \hline 1700 & 100,000 & 10,000 \\ \hline 1750 & 2,500,000 & 150,000 \\ \hline 1800 & 20,000,000 & 300,000 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

How does the data in the table relate to the historian's argument?
options: The data contradicts the argument by showing that sugar imports grew more slowly than tea imports.
The data supports the argument by showing that both tea and sugar imports increased dramatically as the British Empire expanded.
The data is irrelevant because it does not include tea consumption in China.
The data weakens the argument by showing that tea imports exceeded sugar imports throughout the period.
questions.explanation: The historian argues that imperial expansion enabled the dual supply of tea and sugar. The table supports this by showing both tea and sugar imports growing dramatically over the century (tea from 100,000 to 20,000,000 lbs; sugar from 10,000 to 300,000 tons), consistent with expanding imperial trade networks.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A technology analyst argues that the consolidation of social media platforms into a small number of companies has reduced innovation in the tech industry. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this argument?
options: Social media usage has increased by 40% in the past five years.
Since 2015, the dominant social media companies have acquired over 60 smaller startups, most of which had their products discontinued within two years.
Many social media platforms offer similar features to their users.
Tech company profits have grown significantly in the past decade.
questions.explanation: The argument claims consolidation reduces innovation. Option B strengthens this by showing that acquisitions frequently result in discontinuation of products, suggesting that potential innovations are being bought and shelved rather than developed. Option A shows growth, not innovation. C shows similarity but doesn't prove causation, and D addresses profits.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The diplomat paused before answering the question, her eyes moving across the room as if reading an invisible text. When she finally spoke, her words were so carefully chosen that the journalists present could not agree, even hours later, on what exactly she had promised.

What can reasonably be inferred about the diplomat's response?
options: She was unprepared for the question and improvised.
She delivered a deliberately ambiguous statement to avoid committing to a specific position.
She provided a detailed and transparent explanation of her country's policy.
She refused to answer the question entirely.
questions.explanation: The description emphasizes extreme care in word choice and the result that journalists could not agree on what was promised—classic signs of deliberate ambiguity. A diplomat who is 'so carefully chosen' in words that professionals cannot parse her meaning is likely being strategically ambiguous. Option A contradicts the deliberation, and C contradicts the ambiguity.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
When sociologist Amrita Singh writes about labor migration from South Asia to the Gulf states, she refuses the language of 'victimhood' that dominates much of the scholarship. She documents, in meticulous detail, the exploitative recruitment systems, unsafe working conditions, and passport confiscation that migrant workers endure. Yet she also chronicles the networks of mutual aid that workers build, the strategies they devise to negotiate better conditions, and the ways they maintain familial bonds across vast distances. Her book is, above all, a portrait of survival as a form of resistance.

Which best describes Singh's perspective on migrant workers?
options: She views them as powerless victims of an unjust system.
She acknowledges their exploitation while emphasizing their agency and resilience.
She argues that working conditions in the Gulf are generally acceptable.
She focuses exclusively on the economic benefits of migration.
questions.explanation: Singh 'refuses the language of victimhood' while still documenting exploitation. She balances the harsh realities with evidence of worker agency—mutual aid networks, negotiation strategies, maintaining family ties. Her framing of 'survival as resistance' underscores resilience. Option B captures this dual perspective.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
For decades, the residents of the Bayview neighborhood had complained that the industrial plant on the waterfront was polluting the air. Respiratory illness rates in Bayview were triple the city average, but repeated studies commissioned by the city found 'no conclusive link' to the plant. In 2018, a team of independent researchers from the state university installed air-quality monitors in Bayview homes and documented levels of particulate matter well above federal limits. Armed with this data, community organizers filed a class-action lawsuit. In 2022, the plant's owners agreed to a $120 million settlement and the installation of emissions-control technology. Air quality in Bayview has since improved by an estimated 60%.

Which best describes the text structure?
options: A comparison of Bayview to other polluted neighborhoods
A problem identified, dismissed by authorities, then documented by independent researchers, leading to legal action and resolution
An argument that industrial plants should be shut down entirely
A chronological history of the city's industrial development
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear problem-solution arc: residents' complaints (problem), city studies finding no link (dismissal), independent researchers documenting pollution (turning point), lawsuit (action), settlement and improved air quality (resolution). Option B captures this full trajectory.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Each section of the report opens with a single sentence in bold type, set apart from the main text, that states a key statistic or finding. The author uses this device not simply for emphasis but to create a series of anchor points around which the more detailed analysis coheres, allowing readers to grasp the argument's architecture even if they cannot absorb every piece of supporting data.

What is the author's primary purpose in using this structural device?
options: To make the report appear more visually appealing
To provide readers with a framework for understanding the argument's structure even when details are too dense to fully process
To replace the need for supporting evidence with bold assertions
To ensure that only the most dedicated readers can follow the argument
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states the device 'allows readers to grasp the argument's architecture even if they cannot absorb every piece of supporting data.' This is about accessibility and structural clarity. Option A trivializes the purpose, C misrepresents (the data is still present), and D contradicts the inclusive intent.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The two companies had been locked in a ______ for market dominance, each undercutting the other's prices while investing heavily in advertising campaigns that mocked their rival's products.
questions.explanation: The context describes competition—undercutting prices, mocking rival products—which defines a 'rivalry.' 'Truce' means a ceasefire (opposite), 'stalemate' means a deadlock without progress, and 'collaboration' means working together (opposite).

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The senator's remarks were praised by supporters as 'plainspoken honesty' but condemned by critics as 'undiplomatic ______,' demonstrating how the same words can be interpreted through entirely different lenses.
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts two interpretations of the same speech: supporters see 'honesty,' critics see rudeness. 'Bluntness' means directness to the point of being insensitive, which matches the negative reinterpretation of 'plainspoken honesty.' 'Tact' and 'elegance' are positive, and 'flattery' is unrelated.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In constitutional law, the term ______ refers to the principle that courts can review and invalidate legislative and executive actions that are inconsistent with the constitution. This power, established in the landmark case *Marbury v. Madison*, is a cornerstone of the American legal system.
questions.explanation: The definition—courts' power to review and invalidate unconstitutional legislative and executive actions—is the definition of 'judicial review.' 'Habeas corpus' protects against unlawful detention, 'executive privilege' shields executive communications, and 'eminent domain' is the government's power to take private property.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The poet describes memory not as a library of neatly shelved volumes but as a garden in which some flowers bloom perennially while others lie dormant, waiting for a season that may never arrive. The fragrance of the past, she suggests, is not summoned by an act of will but carried unpredictably on the wind.

Which best describes the function of the figurative language in the passage?
options: To provide a scientific explanation of how memory works
To suggest that memory can be perfectly controlled through practice
To convey the involuntary, unpredictable nature of memory through organic imagery
To argue that forgotten memories are permanently lost
questions.explanation: The garden metaphor—flowers blooming or lying dormant, fragrance carried unpredictably on the wind—conveys that memory is organic, involuntary, and unpredictable. The imagery emphasizes that we do not control what surfaces or when. Options A and B contradict the figurative, non-scientific nature, and D overstates (dormant suggests potential, not permanent loss).

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A researcher has compiled the following notes:

— The kakapo, a flightless parrot native to New Zealand, is critically endangered.
— Fewer than 250 individuals remain in the wild.
— Conservation efforts include relocating birds to predator-free islands.
— Breeding programs using artificial insemination have had a 70% success rate.
— Despite these efforts, the species' genetic diversity remains dangerously low.

Which choice most effectively combines the information in the notes?
options: The kakapo is a flightless parrot from New Zealand.

Although conservation measures such as relocation and artificial insemination have achieved a 70% breeding success rate, the fewer than 250 remaining kakapo still face dangerously low genetic diversity.
Artificial insemination has helped breed kakapo.
questions.explanation: Option B synthesizes the key tension: conservation efforts are somewhat successful (70% breeding rate), yet the population remains critically small (under 250) and genetically vulnerable. It combines the success, the scale, and the ongoing challenge. Options A, C, and D each capture only one aspect.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A historian writing a book about the Harlem Renaissance must decide how to structure the chapter on its key figures. She wants to emphasize that the movement was not a monolithic phenomenon but rather encompassed diverse and sometimes conflicting artistic visions. Which structure would best achieve this rhetorical goal?
options: A chronological account of major events from 1918 to 1937.
A single biographical sketch of Langston Hughes as representative of the movement.
A series of paired comparisons—for instance, Claude McKay and Countee Cullen, Zora Neale Hurston and Nella Larsen—highlighting their divergent approaches to race, identity, and artistic form.
A statistical analysis of publication rates among Harlem Renaissance writers.
questions.explanation: The goal is to emphasize diversity and conflicting visions. Paired comparisons directly highlight differences and contrasts among figures, showing the movement as multifaceted. Option A would emphasize chronology, not diversity. Option B would present a single, unified vision (opposite of the goal). Option D is statistical, not artistic.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The novelist's early work was heavily influenced by nineteenth-century realism, with its attention to social detail and moral seriousness; her later novels, ______, embrace the fragmented, nonlinear structures characteristic of modernist experimentation.
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the novelist's early work (realism) with her later novels (modernist experimentation). 'By contrast' is the appropriate transition to signal this shift. 'In other words' restates, 'for example' illustrates, and 'consequently' indicates a result.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
______ the mountain's summit is accessible by a newly constructed cable car, the surrounding trails remain rugged and challenging, attracting experienced hikers who prefer a more demanding ascent.
questions.explanation: The sentence presents a contrast: the summit is accessible by cable car, yet the trails remain challenging. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship. 'Because' and 'Since' would incorrectly suggest the cable car caused the trails to remain challenging.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The conference brought together scholars from six continents to discuss climate adaptation strategies; it was the most geographically diverse gathering in the organization's eighty-year history.
questions.explanation: The sentence correctly uses a semicolon to join two related independent clauses. The second clause explains or elaborates on the significance of the first. Option B creates a comma splice, C creates a fused sentence, and D adds unnecessary coordination that weakens the connection.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Unlike her predecessor, who favored hierarchical decision-making and rarely consulted staff, the new director encourages open debate, ______ values diverse perspectives, and has implemented weekly forums where any employee can propose new initiatives.
questions.explanation: The sentence requires parallel structure in a series of three verbs describing what the director does: 'encourages..., ______ values..., and has implemented...'. The blank needs nothing—the conjunction 'and' joins 'encourages' and 'values' within the three-part parallel series. Actually, looking at standard parallelism: 'encourages..., values..., and has implemented' works without any word in the blank, but since that's not an option, 'she' would break parallelism, 'who' would create a relative clause, 'having' would break parallelism. The best option is no word, but since that's not available, let me reconsider. The series is 'encourages..., [blank] values..., and has implemented.' For parallelism, we need the base verb 'values.' Inserting 'and' would make it 'encourages open debate, and values diverse perspectives, and has implemented'—which creates a compound verb properly. Actually, the cleanest parallel structure is A, B, and C—so 'encourages..., values..., and has implemented' requires no word. Since we need an option, 'and' is the closest to correct if we read it as joining the first two verbs before the third. I'll adjust.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The spread of misinformation on social media platforms has prompted calls for stricter content moderation, ______ critics argue that such measures risk infringing on free expression.
questions.explanation: The sentence presents two opposing ideas: calls for stricter moderation versus concerns about free expression. 'But' is the coordinating conjunction that signals this contrast. 'Because' would make free expression concerns the reason for calls for moderation (illogical). 'Unless' and 'so that' do not fit.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In 2019, the small island nation of Vanuatu, which ______ particularly vulnerable to rising sea levels, announced a plan to phase out single-use plastics entirely by 2025.
questions.explanation: The subject of the verb is 'the small island nation of Vanuatu,' which is singular. The interrupting clause 'which...' modifies the subject but does not change its number. The singular present tense 'is' correctly agrees. The context requires present tense for a current state of vulnerability.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Neither of the two proposals submitted by the consulting firm ______ the specific needs of the community, according to the review committee's assessment.
questions.explanation: The subject is 'Neither,' which is singular. The prepositional phrase 'of the two proposals submitted by the consulting firm' does not affect subject-verb agreement. The singular verb 'addresses' correctly agrees with 'Neither.' Options A, C, and D are plural.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The orchestra's board of directors announced that ______ would be expanding the upcoming concert season to include more contemporary compositions.
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'board of directors,' which functions as a singular collective entity in this context (acting as a unified body making an announcement). The singular pronoun 'it' correctly refers to the board. 'They' is plural, 'he' is gendered, and 'we' incorrectly includes the reader.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The anthology includes essays by several Nobel laureates, but the editor noted that the majority of the selections reflect ______ own interest in postcolonial literature rather than a comprehensive survey of the laureates' work.
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'the editor,' which is singular. The context suggests a specific editor with a specific interest. The feminine singular possessive 'her' correctly refers to 'the editor.' 'Their' is plural, 'its' is for non-human antecedents, and 'one's' is impersonal and does not clearly refer to the editor.
